rationale: The cited paper is discussed multiple times as a central prior study on slow motion in marketing, including in the introduction, theoretical background, and the review table. The citing paper uses Yin et al. (2021) to establish that prior work emphasized cognitive-inference effects (e.g., intentionality and perceived fakeness) and could show negative ad outcomes. It then positions its own contribution as adding a different mechanism (processing fluency) and boundary conditions for when slow motion improves or worsens evaluations. This is a meaningful framing and conceptual setup role, not a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Yin et al. (2021) as key prior evidence about slow motion effects in advertising and extends that line by shifting focus from intentionality-based inference to fluency-based hedonic responses in short-form video. It adds new moderators and evidence showing conditions under which slow motion helps versus hurts consumer evaluations.
